title: Neural networks
text:
Recap


Gradient descent minimizes any differentiable loss
Linear regression is a single linear layer — let’s stack more

title: Neural networks
text:
One unit


A unit computes a weighted sum, then a nonlinearity:

\[h = \sigma(w^\top x + b)\]

Without \(\sigma\), stacking layers collapses back into one linear map
Popular choices: ReLU, sigmoid, tanh

title: Neural networks
text:
A two-layer network

import numpy as np

def relu(z):
    return np.maximum(0, z)

def forward(x, W1, b1, W2, b2):
    h = relu(W1 @ x + b1)     # hidden layer
    return W2 @ h + b2        # output layer

x = np.array([1.0, -0.5])
W1 = np.array([[0.3, -1.2], [0.8, 0.4], [-0.5, 0.9]])
b1 = np.zeros(3)
W2 = np.array([[1.0, -0.7, 0.2]])
b2 = np.zeros(1)
print(forward(x, W1, b1, W2, b2))

title: Neural networks
text:
Training


Same recipe as before: define a loss, follow its gradient
Backpropagation computes the gradient layer by layer via the chain rule
Frameworks (PyTorch, JAX) automate this — you write only forward







Tip


If training stalls, check the learning rate first, then the data pipeline. The model is rarely the problem.

title: Machine Learning 101
text:
Machine Learning 101


A hands-on introduction to machine learning — 4 lectures plus reference material. Sample course for the CleanSlideKit theme.









Foundations



Getting started
Gradient descent



Models



Linear regression
Neural networks



Reference



Cheat sheet

title: Getting started
text:
What is machine learning?


Programs that improve from data instead of hand-written rules
Three main flavors:

Supervised learning — learn from labeled examples
Unsupervised learning — find structure without labels
Reinforcement learning — learn from rewards









Vocabulary


A model is a function with tunable parameters; training means finding parameters that fit the data.

title: Getting started
text:
The workflow


Collect and clean data
Split into train / test sets
Fit a model on the training set
Evaluate on the held-out test set


Never evaluate on the data you trained on — that only measures memorization.

title: Getting started
text:
Overfitting


A model can be too flexible: it memorizes noise instead of learning signal
Symptom: great on training data, poor on new data
Cure: more data, simpler models, regularization

title: Getting started
text: Your first experiment

title: Getting started
text:
Hands-on: iris dataset


Train a classifier on the iris dataset and report its test accuracy.

from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, random_state=0)

clf = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=3)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(clf.score(X_test, y_test))

title: Getting started
text:
Where to go next


Gradient descent — how models actually learn
Linear regression — the simplest useful model
scikit-learn user guide — excellent worked examples

title: Cheat sheet
text:
Formulas


Mean squared error: \(L = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i (\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2\)
Gradient step: \(\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla L\)
Ridge objective: \(\|Xw - y\|^2 + \lambda\|w\|^2\)

title: Cheat sheet
text:
Rules of thumb


Always keep a held-out test set — no exceptions
Diverging loss → learning rate too large
Great train / poor test accuracy → overfitting: simplify or regularize
Tune hyperparameters on a validation split, never the test set

title: Gradient descent
text:
Recap


Last time: fitting a line by solving the normal equation directly
Works great — until the model has millions of parameters
Today: an iterative recipe that scales

title: Gradient descent
text:
The idea


Standing on a hillside in fog, you can still walk downhill: feel the slope under your feet, step against it
The loss \(L(\theta)\) is the hill; the gradient \(\nabla L\) is the slope

\[\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, \nabla L(\theta_t)\]

The learning rate \(\eta\) controls the step size

title: Gradient descent
text:
A minimal implementation

import numpy as np

def gradient_descent(grad, theta0, lr=0.1, steps=100):
    """Minimize a function given its gradient."""
    theta = np.asarray(theta0, dtype=float)
    history = [theta.copy()]
    for _ in range(steps):
        theta -= lr * grad(theta)
        history.append(theta.copy())
    return theta, np.array(history)

# minimize f(x, y) = x^2 + 10 y^2
grad = lambda t: np.array([2 * t[0], 20 * t[1]])
theta, path = gradient_descent(grad, theta0=[9.0, 2.0], lr=0.08)
print(theta)          # -> close to [0, 0]
print(len(path))      # -> 101 positions visited

title: Gradient descent
section: Tweak Watch it succeed — and fail
text:
Tweak Watch it succeed — and fail

Re-run the code, changing one thing at a time:








Goal
Edit




Take bigger steps
lr=0.08 → lr=0.15


Overshoot and diverge
lr=0.08 → lr=0.25


Start from somewhere else
theta0=[9.0, 2.0] → theta0=[-4.0, 1.5]


Run longer
steps=100 → steps=1000










Tip


Diverging loss almost always means the learning rate is too large. Halve it and try again.

title: Gradient descent
text: Beyond vanilla gradient descent

title: Gradient descent
text:
Variants


Stochastic gradient descent — estimate the gradient from a mini-batch
Momentum — keep a running average of past gradients
Adam — per-parameter adaptive step sizes; the default in deep learning








Quiz


Why does mini-batching make each step cheaper but noisier?

title: Gradient descent
text:
Further reading


Ruder (2016), An overview of gradient descent optimization algorithms
The neural networks lecture builds directly on this

title: Linear regression
text:
The model


Predict a number as a weighted sum of inputs:

\[\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \dots + w_d x_d + b\]

Fitting = choosing \(w, b\) that minimize the mean squared error

\[L(w, b) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right)^2\]

title: Linear regression
text:
The normal equation


For linear regression the minimum has a closed form:

\[\hat{w} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y\]

One line of algebra, one line of code — no iteration needed
Cost is \(O(d^3)\) in the number of features: fine for hundreds, hopeless for millions

title: Linear regression
text:
In code

import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
X = rng.normal(size=(200, 3))
y = X @ np.array([2.0, -1.0, 0.5]) + 0.1 * rng.normal(size=200)

# normal equation with a bias column
Xb = np.hstack([X, np.ones((200, 1))])
w = np.linalg.solve(Xb.T @ Xb, Xb.T @ y)
print(w)   # -> [ 2.0, -1.0, 0.5, ~0.0 ]

title: Linear regression
text:
Regularization


With many features, plain least squares overfits
Ridge regression shrinks weights toward zero:

\[L(w) = \|Xw - y\|^2 + \lambda \|w\|^2\]

The penalty strength λ is a hyperparameter — tune it on a validation set, never on the test set.
